did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed an expert system for skin cancer detection (a dermatology task) using the Forward Chaining inference method and Certainty Factor (CF) technique, which are computational modeling methods derived from artificial intelligence research.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original work involving the design and development of a desktop-based expert system for skin cancer detection using Forward Chaining and Certainty Factor methods. It includes a 'Research Method' section detailing system modeling, implementation, and testing procedures, and a 'Results' section reporting a 100% accuracy rate from testing the system against expert diagnoses. Although published in a conference proceedings, it contains original experimental results and system validation rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Potential Level Detection of Skin Cancer with Expert System Using Forward Chaining and Certainty Factor Method
journal: The 2018 IEEE International Conference on Internet of Things and Intelligence System (IoTaIS)
brief_summary: This study developed a desktop-based expert system for the early detection of skin cancer potential levels using the Forward Chaining inference method and Certainty Factor technique. The system utilizes a knowledge base derived from dermatologist experts, incorporating symptoms such as mole itching, bleeding, and family history to assess risk. Users input symptoms via a user interface, and the system calculates a certainty factor to classify the condition as high, medium, or low risk. The evaluation approach involved testing the system's diagnostic accuracy against expert diagnoses. The results demonstrated a 100% accuracy rate, indicating the system produces conclusions identical to those of medical experts. The authors conclude that the system is feasible for helping users perform early detection of skin cancer.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study focuses on the detection of potential levels of skin cancer, specifically assessing the condition of moles (nevus) to determine if they are high, medium, or low risk for developing into skin cancer. The system uses symptoms related to moles to predict this risk.

The expert system assesses the condition of the patient's nevus (mole) based on symptoms such as itching, bleeding, pain, hair growth, and changes in shape/size. The output is an assessment of the nevus condition (high, medium, or low risk) as an early warning for skin cancer.

Mentioned in the Related Work section as a major class of skin cancer that is malignant and metastasizes, but not explicitly used as a diagnostic class or target label in the proposed expert system's output classes.
Mentioned in the Related Work section as a type of non-melanoma skin cancer, but not used as a specific diagnostic class in the study.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer potential level (High Risk, Medium Risk, or Low Risk)
all_task_types.details: The expert system classifies the condition of a nevus (mole) into one of three discrete diagnostic categories: High Risk, Medium Risk, or Low Risk, based on user-reported symptoms and expert-derived rules using Forward Chaining and Certainty Factor methods.

datasets_used.name: Expert-derived Knowledge Base (Symptom Rules)
datasets_used.description: A knowledge base constructed through literature study and interviews with dermatologist experts, containing rules and Certainty Factor (CF) weights for 10 specific symptoms related to skin cancer potential levels (High, Medium, Low risk). This is not a public or private image dataset but a rule-based dataset used to build the expert system.

use.training: Used to define the IF-THEN rules and CF weights for the expert system's inference engine.
use.validation: Used for accuracy testing by comparing system outputs against expert diagnoses.
use.testing: Used for accuracy testing by comparing system outputs against expert diagnoses.
use.other: The system was tested against 3 specific case scenarios (examples) provided by the authors to verify calculation accuracy and diagnostic alignment with experts.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing; it reported a 100% accuracy rate by comparing system outputs against expert diagnoses for a small number of case scenarios.

Expert-derived Knowledge Base (Symptom R.notes: The system was tested against 3 specific case scenarios (examples) provided by the authors to verify calculation accuracy and diagnostic alignment with experts. The results showed that the system produced the same conclusions as medical experts. Additionally, Certainty Factor (CF) calculations were verified: manual calculation for a Medium Risk case yielded 88%, matching the application's output of 88%. A High Risk case example yielded a CF of 0.9997 (97.77%). A Low Risk case example yielded a CF of 0.952 (95%).

architecture_used.main_model: Expert System using Forward Chaining inference method and Certainty Factor (CF) technique

architecture_details.knowledge_base_source: Expert-derived rules and CF weights for 10 symptoms (itching, bleeding, pain, hair growth, sunlight exposure, hot/burning sensation, non-healing sores, abnormal weight loss, shape/size change, family history of cancer)

training_details.dataset: Expert-derived knowledge base (symptom rules and CF weights)

classification_or_output_head.mechanism: Certainty Factor calculation based on user input (YES/NO) and expert-defined weights, mapped to risk levels

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Expert System using Forward Chaining and Certainty Factor Method
conclusions: The expert system utilizing Forward Chaining and Certainty Factor methods is feasible for implementation in mobile applications to detect potential levels of skin cancer.
The system achieved a 100% accuracy rate when its diagnostic outputs were compared against expert diagnoses, indicating it produces results identical to medical experts.
The Certainty Factor calculations within the system were verified to be accurate, matching manual calculations for tested cases.
The system is designed to help users, including the general public and doctors, assess the condition of moles (nevus) and facilitate early detection of skin cancer.
important_remarks: The reported 100% accuracy is based on an extremely small validation set of only 3 case scenarios, which is insufficient for robust statistical evaluation.
The study lacks external validation; the system was tested only against expert diagnoses for specific examples rather than a broader, independent clinical dataset.
The knowledge base relies on subjective Certainty Factor weights assigned by experts rather than objective, data-driven learning, introducing potential bias.
The system is described as 'desktop-based' in the methodology but mentions smartphone camera access in the system overview, creating ambiguity regarding the deployment platform.

The study focuses on 'potential levels' (risk stratification) of moles rather than definitive diagnosis of specific skin cancer types (e.g., melanoma vs. basal cell carcinoma), limiting its direct clinical diagnostic utility.
No statistical significance tests or confidence intervals were reported for the accuracy results.
The system requires users to manually input symptoms via YES/NO questions, which may introduce user-reporting bias compared to automated image analysis.
